coredata.dc:title: Enhanced carbon quantum dots-based chemiluminescence probes for copper ion detection in human plasma and urine
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of The editorial office of Journal of Materials Science & Technology.
coredata.prism:publisher: Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of The editorial office of Journal of Materials Science & Technology.
coredata.dc:creator: Lee, Seul-Yi
Sim, Nicole
Gautam, Jagadis
Kim, Young-Teck
Park, Soo-Jin
Lee, Ji Hoon
coredata.dc:description: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs), synthesized via the rapid pyrolysis of ammonium citrate dibasic and ethylenediamine dihydrochloride, demonstrate remarkable chemiluminescence capabilities, emitting a vibrant blue-green light in peroxyoxalate chemiluminescence (PO-CL) reactions. Among these, a distinct variant of N-CQDs is an exceptionally sensitive biosensor, facilitating the swift detection of copper ions (Cu2+) within human plasma and urine samples. Upon rapid binding of N-CQDs and Cu2+, non-emissive complex forms due to intra-chemiluminescence resonance energy transfer (intra-CRET) initiated by the PO-CL reaction. Consequently, the brightness of the biosensor diminishes proportionally with increasing Cu2+ concentrations in human samples. Featuring a low sensing limit of 19.5 nM and an expansive dynamic range spanning from 0.05 to 3.8 µM, this biosensor empowers the rapid and precise quantification of trace Cu2+ levels with exceptional accuracy and recovery rates. In alignment with copper quantification guidelines set forth by the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) and the National Institutes of Health (NIH), this selective biosensor stands as a cutting-edge monitoring tool, poised to advance analytical capabilities in various fields.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs)
Peroxyoxalate chemiluminescence (PO-CL)
Chemiluminescence resonance energy transfer (CRET)
Biosensor
Photoluminescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Surface and chemical characterization of N-CQDs
Accuracy, precision, and recovery analysis of the biosensor
Correlation analysis of novel biosensor and conventional method
Analysis of the interaction of N-CQDs and Cu2+
Analysis of the binding reaction of N-CQDs and Cu2+ in human plasma
Accuracy, precision, recovery, and selectivity analysis of the biosensors
Correlation analysis of the novel biosensor and a conventional biosensor

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This project was performed as part of Luminescent MD, LLC's Research Program (LMD-201) and was supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Korea government (MSIT) (No. 2023R1A2C1004109 and 2022R1I1A1A01070007). Also, this was supported by Korea Energy (No. 2024-Research and Development in Field Technology, Yeongheung-01).

This project was performed as part of Luminescent MD, LLC's Research Program (LMD-201) and was supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) Grant funded by the Korean Government (MSIT) (No. 2022M3J7A1062940 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a–d) A high-resolution TEM and corresponding mapping images of N-CQDs, (e) size distribution of N-CQDs observed with DLS, (f) FTIR spectrum of N-CQDs, (g) C 1s spectrum of N-CQDs, (h) O 1s spectrum of N-CQDs, (i) N 1s spectrum of N-CQDs, (j) atomic concentration of N-CQDs, (k) XRD spectrum and (l) body-centered cubic structure of N-CQDs.

(a) Peroxyoxalate chemiluminescence (PO-CL) and fluorescence excited with LED (365 nm) of N-CQDs. 1: bis(2,4,6-trichlorophenyl) oxalate (TCPO), 2: Imidazole. (b) PO-CL spectrum of N-CQDs obtained with a charge-coupled device, (c) fluorescence spectrum of N-CQDs (excitation wavelength: 365 nm). (d) PO-CL reaction measured in the presence of N-CQDs and absence of N-CQDs and Cu2+ and the complex formed from the binding reaction of N-CQD and Cu2+.

Chemiluminescence intensity of N-CQDs: Effects of (a) TCPO content, (b) imidazole content, (c) H2O2 content, (d) pH (10 mM), and (e) buffer solution and pH; (f) Chemiluminescence spectra of N-CQDs in the absence and presence of 3 M Cu2+; (g) CL0/CL of N-CQD in the absence (CL0) and presence (CL) of Cu2+; (h) CL0/CL of resorufin in the absence (CL0) and presence (CL) of Cu2+; (i) Chemiluminescence intensity of N-CQDs content.

(a) Binding reaction of N-CQDs and Cu2+ in supernatant obtained after the centrifugation of human plasma, (b) binding reaction of N-CQDs and Cu2+ in human urine, (c) determination of incubation time for the binding reaction of N-CQDs and Cu2+ in supernatant obtained after the centrifugation of human plasma, (d, e) calibration curves capable of quantifying Cu2+ in a sample, (f, g) selectivity of the new method capable of quantifying Cu2+. [Cu2+] = 2 µM, [other heavy metals] = 20 µM, [alkali metals or alkali earth metals] = 2 mM, [creatinine, urea, or uric acid] = 1 mM, [albumin or IgG] = 1 mg/mL. (h) correlation of new method and chromogenic method for the quantification of Cu2+ in human plasma, (i) correlation of new method and chromogenic method for the quantification of Cu2+ in human urine.
Illustration of accuracy, precision, and recovery of the biosensor capable of quantifying Cu2+ in human plasma (N=5).

head.ce:title: Enhanced carbon quantum dots-based chemiluminescence probes for copper ion detection in human plasma and urine

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Advanced Materials Engineering for Information and Electronics

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Advanced Materials Engineering for Information and Electronics, Kyung Hee University, Yongin 17104, Republic of Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs), synthesized via the rapid pyrolysis of ammonium citrate dibasic and ethylenediamine dihydrochloride, demonstrate remarkable chemiluminescence capabilities, emitting a vibrant blue-green light in peroxyoxalate chemiluminescence (PO-CL) reactions. Among these, a distinct variant of N-CQDs is an exceptionally sensitive biosensor, facilitating the swift detection of copper ions (Cu2+) within human plasma and urine samples. Upon rapid binding of N-CQDs and Cu2+, non-emissive complex forms due to intra-chemiluminescence resonance energy transfer (intra-CRET) initiated by the PO-CL reaction. Consequently, the brightness of the biosensor diminishes proportionally with increasing Cu2+ concentrations in human samples. Featuring a low sensing limit of 19.5 nM and an expansive dynamic range spanning from 0.05 to 3.8 µM, this biosensor empowers the rapid and precise quantification of trace Cu2+ levels with exceptional accuracy and recovery rates. In alignment with copper quantification guidelines set forth by the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) and the National Institutes of Health (NIH), this selective biosensor stands as a cutting-edge monitoring tool, poised to advance analytical capabilities in various fields.

Traditional fluorescence-based methods often encounter challenges with background noise, but the nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQD) sensor, relying on peroxyoxalate chemiluminescence (PO-CL), minimizes interference, enhancing precision in monitoring copper ions (Cu2+) and other toxic heavy metals. This advancement aligns with sustainable practices, fostering a healthier balance between technological progress and environmental stewardship.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) represent a class of carbon-based nanomaterials characterized by their diminutive size, typically measuring below 10 nm, and distinguished by their remarkable optical, electronic, and chemical properties [1–5]. CQDs serve as indispensable entities across a spectrum of scientific and technological frontiers, driving innovations in areas ranging from biosensors for fluorescence-based detection to cellular imaging and drug delivery within the expansive realms of biotechnology, chemistry, life sciences, and materials engineering [6,7]. A plethora of studies underscore their ubiquity in these domains, emphasizing their key advantages, including low toxicity, customizable photoluminescence, exceptional water solubility, chemical inertness, and versatile surface functionalization capabilities [8–10].

The synthesis of CQDs encompasses diverse methodologies, each offering distinct advantages and challenges. Hydrothermal synthesis emerges as an environmentally benign approach amenable to scalable production [11]. Laser ablation yields highly crystalline CQDs, albeit with limitations such as modest yields and the necessity for expensive equipment [12,13]. Conversely, microwave-assisted pyrolysis stands out for its rapid synthesis kinetics and precise modulation of CQD size and properties, rendering it particularly suited for large-scale manufacturing endeavors [14,15].

CQDs are crucial as fluorescent probes in sensors designed to detect toxic heavy metals, such as copper, mercury, and lead, in drinking water, rivers, and oceans [16–18]. Chen et al. synthesized 2 nm CQDs with a body-centered cubic (C8) structure via sucrose carbonization, confirmed by high-resolution TEM and X-ray diffraction. These CQDs exhibit dual-band luminescence at 432 nm and 520 nm, with blue emission linked to surface defects and green emission attributed to C=O-related defects [19]. The effectiveness of these sensors relies heavily on the surface properties of CQDs, which can be optimized through heteroatom doping. Nitrogen-doped CQDs (N-CQDs) have shown exceptional selectivity and sensitivity in sensing applications [20–22]. Zhao et al. developed highly luminescent N-CQDs via a one-step hydrothermal method using chitosan as a carbon and nitrogen source [23]. These N-CQDs exhibit excitation-dependent fluorescence, with peaks at 330 nm (excitation) and 410 nm (emission). Due to Fe3+ ion quenching, they function as sensitive fluorescent sensors for Fe3+ detection, with a detection limit of 0.15 µM. The sensor displays excellent selectivity for Fe3+, even in the presence of other metal ions, and performs effectively in natural water samples, suggesting potential for monitoring other trace elements [23].

Copper, an indispensable trace element essential for human physiology, can pose serious health hazards in excessive concentrations. The risk of toxicity often arises from elevated copper levels in water sources, predominantly attributed to copper-containing pipelines, fixtures, or alloys that facilitate copper leaching. Furthermore, accidental ingestion of copper salts, dermal exposure to copper-infused topical treatments, or uncoated copper cookware for acidic food preparation can contribute to copper toxicity. Manifestations of severe symptoms range from gastrointestinal distress and headaches to renal dysfunction, with extreme cases culminating in fatality [24,25].

To address copper toxicity, regulatory bodies such as the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) have implemented guidelines to mitigate risks, setting recommended limits of 1.3 mg/L for copper in public water systems. Concurrently, the National Institutes of Health (NIH) advises that daily copper intake for adults should not surpass 10 mg [26]. Building on contemporary understanding, establishing an oral reference dose of 0.04 mg Cu kg−1 d−1 safeguards against acute or chronic toxicity in adults and children [26]. Therapeutic strategies encompass a multifaceted approach, including reducing copper absorption, vigilant patient monitoring, providing supportive care, and implementing chelation therapy. Zinc administration in the early stages can effectively delay symptom onset by competitively inhibiting copper absorption in the gastrointestinal tract. Regular surveillance of copper levels within the body is imperative to avert adverse health outcomes.

Most fluorescent probes function as emitters, producing bright chemiluminescence by capturing energy from a high-energy intermediate generated during a peroxyoxalate chemiluminescence (PO-CL) reaction based on the principle of chemically induced electron exchange luminescence (CIEEL) [27,28]. Biosensors utilizing emitters with PO-CL detection offer significant benefits, including stable chemiluminescence and high quantum efficiency, outperforming other chemiluminescent systems and showing great promise for target analysis. A typical PO-CL reaction involves oxalates, such as TCPO, reacting with H2O2 as the oxidant and catalyst, producing light by exciting added fluorescent molecules [29]. This method is often 10 to 100 times more sensitive than fluorescence-based sensors. This superior sensitivity arises from the lower background noise in PO-CL, eliminating the need for additional electrical equipment like high-voltage power supplies for LEDs or lasers [28,30–32]. For example, Tan et al. developed a PO-CL biosensor for Cu²⁺ detection using N-doped graphene oxide nanosheets (N-GONs) and an oligopeptide. N-GONs, synthesized via a hydrothermal method with citric acid and ammonia, act as CL enhancers. N-GONs produce strong CL emissions in the PO-CL system, quenched by the oligopeptide due to their interaction. When Cu²⁺ is introduced, the quenched CL gradually recovers, enabling ultra-sensitive detection of Cu²⁺ in water samples, with a detection limit as low as 0.2 pmol L−1, three orders of magnitude better than other CL methods. The eco-friendly components (N-GONs and oligopeptide) make this method promising for Cu2+ monitoring in various samples [33].

Based on the above motivations, we have innovatively synthesized N-CQDs via a swift microwave-assisted pyrolysis reaction, merging ammonium citrate dibasic (ACD) and ethylenediamine dihydrochloride (EDA). Our groundbreaking study unveils the remarkable potential of N-CQDs as superior emitters in PO-CL reactions, outperforming conventional fluorescent dyes. Notably, a distinct variant of N-CQDs exhibits rapid and selective binding affinity towards copper ions (Cu2+), forming a non-chemiluminescent complex within PO-CL reactions. We engineered an exceptionally sensitive biosensor featuring PO-CL detection methodology to leverage the discernible optical contrasts between the N-CQD variant and the resultant complex. This enables precise monitoring of Cu2+ levels in human plasma and urine samples. Our methodology adheres rigorously to EPA and NIH guidelines [26], underscoring its potential for widespread application in environmental and biomedical analyses. Our methodology is further elucidated below.

ACD and EDA were acquired from Millipore Sigma. Bis (2,4,6-trichlorophenyl) oxalate and imidazole were bought from TCI America. All heavy metals used in this research were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Potassium chloride, sodium chloride, calcium chloride, magnesium chloride, urea, and uric acid were purchased from VWR. Human serum albumin, creatinine, and immunoglobulin (IgG) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. H2O2, Tris–HCl buffer, ethyl acetate (spectroscopy grade), and isopropyl alcohol (HPLC grade) were bought from VWR. Double distilled water (DI) of 18.3 mΩ was employed in this study. Centrifugal filters were purchased from Pall Corporation. Pooled human plasma containing citrate as a coagulant agent and pooled human urine were purchased from Innovative Research. Human plasma and urine samples were also provided from Meritus Health in Hagerstown, Maryland, United States.

ACD and EDA were used as precursors to synthesize N-CQDs. 0.2 g of ACD and 0.1 g of EDA were dissolved in 3 ml water. The mixture in a 20-ml borosilicate vial was put into a microwave (1000 W). Then, the mixture was heated for 2 min to synthesize N-CQDs using the one-step microwave-assisted pyrolysis method. 20 mg of products obtained after the 2-min reaction was dissolved in deionized water (1 mL) in a microcentrifuge tube. The solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min to separate large sizes of by-products. The solution that did not contain large by-products was diluted 10 times with deionized water. Then, 500 µL was transferred into a centrifugal filter (MWCO; 30k). The solution in the centrifugal filter was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm to remove larger particles than MWCO 30k. The nanoparticles obtained from the centrifugation transferred into a centrifugal filter (MWCO: 10K) were centrifugated at 10,000 rpm for 10 min. Finally, the CQDs (MWCO: 3 k–10 k) were obtained after the centrifugation with the centrifugal filter (MWCO: 3k) to remove non-reacting precursors. The N-CQDs prepared were diluted with deionized water (500 µL). The stock inserted in a black microcentrifuge tube was stored at ambient conditions.

As a working solution, the N-CQDs stored in the black microcentrifuge tube were diluted 800 times in 10 mM Tris–HCl (pH 8.5). The working solution (10 µL) was inserted into a borosilicate tube (12 mm × 75 mm). The tube was put into the detection window of the luminometer (Lumat 9507, Berthold Inc.). The mixture (25 µL) of H2O2 (50 mM) and imidazole (0.2 mM) dispersed in isopropyl alcohol was distributed via a first syringe pump of the luminometer. Then, intense red light was radiated instantly with the addition of 0.5 mM TCPO (25 µL) consuming the second syringe pump, and it was immediately assessed for 10 s.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface and chemical characterization of N-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: To study the surface properties of N-CQDs, 4 different instruments (e.g., Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), high-resolution Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), dynamic light scattering (DLS)) were used. FT-IR (Agilent Technologies, USA) was utilized to examine the functional groups formed on the surface of CQDs employing a Varian Attenuated Total Reflectance (ATR) FT-IR. TEM images were acquired with a JEOL 2100 field-emission transmission electron microscope (JEOL Co., Japan), performing at an acceleration voltage of 200 kV. The surface chemical composition of the samples containing CQDs was analyzed by applying a VersaProbe III Scanning XPS Microprobe (Physical Electronics, USA). The particle sizes (hydrodynamic diameters) of CQDs were determined in DI water with dynamic light scattering (DLS) techniques utilizing a Zetasizer ZSP (Marvern Instrument Ltd., UK).

To obtain a linear calibration curve to quantify Cu2+ in a sample, 9 standards containing different concentrations of Cu2+ (Concentration range: 0.05–3.8 µM) in 1 × PBS (pH 7.4). 800-fold diluted N-CQDs from the stock in 10 mM Tris–HCl (pH 8.5) were used. 50 µL of each standard or human plasma was mixed with the working N-CQD solution (50 µL). The mixture was incubated for 5 min at room temperature (21 ± 2 °C). After the incubation, the brightness of light emitted from each mixture was measured with the luminometer with two syringe pumps for 20 s. Finally, an excellent linear calibration curve was obtained with the chemiluminescence measurements of 9 standards to quantify Cu2+ in a sample.

Plasma samples were centrifuged at 14,000 rpm for 5 min. The supernatant obtained after the centrifugation was used to quantify Cu2+ in human plasma. Urine samples were used without any pre-treatment, such as centrifugation and filtration. The linear calibration curve quantifies free copper (Cu2+) in human plasma and urine samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Accuracy, precision, and recovery analysis of the biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: To study the biosensor's accuracy, precision, and recovery, an accurate concentration of Cu2+ was added to a human fluidic sample received from Meritus Health. The mixture of a sample (50 µL) and the working solution (50 µL) diluted 800-fold with the N-CQDs stock was incubated for 5 min at room temperature before the chemiluminescence measurement. Each sample was analyzed five times repeatedly to determine the biosensor's accuracy, precision, and recovery.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Correlation analysis of novel biosensor and conventional method
ce:section.ce:para: To scrutinize the relationship between the new biosensor and a conventional method for quantifying Cu2+ in human plasma and urine, a copper assay kit operated with chromogenic substrates capable of binding Cu2+ was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Supernatants obtained from the centrifugation of human plasma samples (n = 15) were used to quantify Cu2+ in human plasma. Human urine samples (n = 15) were used with no pre-treatment. The correlation curves were obtained with quantifications of samples using two different methods.

Fig. 1 presents morphological properties and structural features analysis of the N-CQDs using various techniques. The high-resolution TEM image (Fig. 1(a)) highlights the presence of N-CQDs with diverse sizes, a product of the rapid and straightforward microwave-assisted pyrolysis method. The corresponding mapping images (Fig. 1(b–d)) reveal the uniform distribution of carbon (C), nitrogen (N), and oxygen (O) atoms across the N-CQDs. DLS measurements (Fig. 1(e)) confirmed size variations of N-CQDs ranging from 0.5 to 7.5 nm, with a dominant size of 1.76 nm within the mixture. FTIR spectrum (Fig. 1(f)) exhibits characteristic peaks associated with various functional groups on N-CQDs' surface, including C=O, C–N, C=C, OH, and N–H. XPS analysis (Fig. 1(g–i)) provides further insights into the chemical composition and bonding interactions of N-CQDs [34]. The C 1s spectrum (Fig. 1(g)) indicates the presence of C=C (284.5 eV), C–C (285.4 eV), C–O (286.5 eV), C=O (288 eV), and C–N (289 eV) bonding interactions on the N-CQD surface. The O 1s spectrum (Fig. 1(h)) confirms the existence of C–O functional groups, while the N 1s spectrum (Fig. 1(i)) reveals the presence of C–N (398 eV), C=N (399 eV), and N–O (400 eV) bonding. As depicted in Fig. 1(j), N-CQDs comprise C, O, N, and Cl elements, with an O/C ratio of approximately 0.37. The minimal presence of Cl is attributed to ethylenediamine dihydrochloride as a precursor in the production of N-CQDs [35,36]. Moreover, the XRD spectrum confirmed the highly crystalline nature of the N-CQDs, displaying a body-centered cubic (C8) structure consistent with previous reports (Fig. 1(k–l)). These findings validate that the N-CQDs, synthesized via pyrolysis of ACD and EDA, possess high crystallinity [19,23].

Fig. 2 (a) displays the bright light emission by N-CQDs, driven by the PO-CL reaction mechanism. Specifically, these CQDs, when excited by the high energy intermediate (X) generated from the reaction of bis(2,4,6-trichlorophenyl) oxalate (TCPO) and H2O2 in the presence of imidazole, emit vivid blue-green light, indicating their suitability as practical emitters in the PO-CL reaction. However, due to the short lifetime of X, it is challenging to determine its precise structure. Thus, based on the experimental results, we analyzed different intermediate structures of X formed in the PO-CL reaction in the presence of imidazole. The relative chemiluminescence analysis suggested that increased CQD concentration proportionally enhanced the chemiluminescence intensity.

Fig. 2(a) illustrates that the chemiluminescence (CL) color emitted by N-CQDs differs from the fluorescence observed under LED excitation at 365 nm. This difference is attributed to the distinct chemical and physical characteristics of X compared to conventional light sources like lasers, LEDs, and Xenon lamps [17]. These visual observations are consistent with the spectral data in Fig. 2(b) and (c). In Fig. 2(b), the CL spectrum of N-CQDs during the PO-CL reaction shows a peak at 475 nm, indicating strong chemiluminescence, while the fluorescence spectrum (Fig. 2(c)) peaks at 441 nm, marking strong fluorescence emission [37,38]. The CL spectrum was captured using a charge-coupled device (CCD) sensor over the 400–800 nm range. Still, due to the lower sensitivity of the CCD compared to a photomultiplier tube (PMT), the data in Fig. 2(b) contains higher noise.

Fig. 3 (a) demonstrates that the optimal TCPO concentration of the PO-CL reaction was determined to be 0.5 mM in the presence of CQDs. The brightness of N-CQDs diminished with the addition of 0.75 and 1 mM TCPO due to self-quenching [39,40] caused by rapid collisions between an overabundance of excited N-CQD particles (N-CQD*) without emission, resulting from excessive 1,2-dioxetanedione (X) formation in the presence of TCPO concentrations above 0.5 mM. Fig. 3(b) reveals that the most effective concentration of imidazole in the PO-CL reaction was 0.2 mM. As imidazole concentration increased to 0.2 mM, the CL brightness intensified and diminished beyond this concentration due to the self-quenching effects. The optimal H2O2 concentration added to the PO-CL reaction was identified as 50 mM (Fig. 3(c)). The brightness of N-CQDs remained consistent across a wide H2O2 concentration range (50–200 mM), supporting the development of a cost-efficient analytical method. Fig. 3(d) shows that N-CQDs were prepared in Tris–HCl (pH 8.5) to attain vivid chemiluminescence. The CL intensities of N-CQDs at pH 9 and 9.5 were inferior to those at pH 8.5, attributable to the diminished efficiency of the PO-CL reaction under primary conditions compared to neutral conditions [41–43]. Additionally, Fig. 3(e) highlights N-CQD brightness's dependence on the buffer type and pH. Tris–HCl (pH 8.5) emerged as the preferred buffer for obtaining enhanced CL emission from N-CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of the interaction of N-CQDs and Cu2+
ce:section.ce:para:
We found that the chemiluminescence emitted by N-CQDs in the absence of Cu2+ surpasses that in the presence of 3 µM Cu2+ as shown in Fig. 3(f). These findings unequivocally indicate that Cu2+ functions as a potent quencher, effectively suppressing the CL emission of N-CQDs within the PO-CL reaction. The CL spectrum is the same in the absence and presence of 3 mM Cu2+. This consistency indicates that Cu2+ acts as a dynamic quencher. Additionally, it is possible that the complex developed from the binding of N-CQD and Cu2+ is a static quencher, as it does not emit CL in the PO-CL reaction. The second-order curve (Fig. 3(g)) was obtained by adding different Cu2+ concentrations. This curve signifies that Cu2+ is a static and dynamic quencher within the PO-CL reaction involving N-CQDs. The second-order curve illustrates those complex forms through the binding interaction between Cu2+ and N-CQDs, acting as a static quencher in the PO-CL reaction when N-CQDs are present. Simultaneously, free Cu2+ remaining after the binding reaction with N-CQDs operates as a dynamic quencher under these reaction conditions. Consequently, the equation governing the second-order curve is derived as follows.
(1)
CL
0
/
CL
=
(
K
S
[ Q ]
+ 1 )
(
K
D
[ Q ]
+ 1 )
where CL0 and CL are relative CL intensities in the absence and presence of Cu2+, K S is the static quenching constant, K D is the dynamic quenching constant, [Q] is the concentration of Cu2+.

As shown in Fig. 3(h),
Cu2+ unctions as a dynamic quencher within the PO-CL reaction when resorufin is present, as indicated in Scheme S1 in supporting information. In this context, resorufin is unable to engage in binding interactions with Cu2+, a departure from the conditions described in a prior report. Consequently, the introduction of Cu2+ yields a linear curve, further characterized using Eq. (2) for fitting:
(2)
CL
0
/
CL
=
(
K
D
[ Q ]
+ 1 )

The concentration range of Cu2+ to obtain the second-order curve shown in Fig. 3(g) was 0 to 12.5 µM, whereas the range for establishing the linear curve presented in Fig. 3(h) extended from 0 to 500 µM. The results indicate that the complex formed by the binding process of Cu2+ and N-CQD serves as a robust static quencher. Thus, we anticipate that the static quenching effect of this complex predominates in the PO-CL reaction, particularly in the presence of relatively low Cu2+ concentrations. As evidence of this, we obtained a well-fitted linear curve for CL0/CL with Cu2+ concentrations lower than 3.13 µM (see Fig. S1), demonstrating the prevalence of dynamic quenching by Cu2+ that remains after forming the complexes at higher Cu2+ concentrations than 3.13 µM. We hypothesize that the complex formation occurs through the binding interaction between Cu2+ and the amine groups located on the surface of N-CQDs, as previously reported in studies involving Cu2+ and N-CQDs synthesized from various precursors [38,44,45]. Scheme S2 outlines a concise reaction mechanism illustrating the formation of this complex through the reaction between Cu2+ and N-CQD.

The findings illustrated in Fig. 3(f) and (g) suggested that the complex cannot emit light in the PO-CL reaction, as indicated by the reaction mechanism outlined in Fig. 2(d). Thus, we anticipate that the proximity between Cu2+ and N-CQD within this complex is less than 10 nm. It becomes plausible that within the complex, the N-CQD, when excited by the high-energy intermediate generated during the PO-CL reaction, transfers energy to Cu2+ through intra-chemiluminescence resonance energy transfer (Intra-CRET) before the N-CQD emits light. This phenomenon aligns with the principles of CRET, which occur when the distance between a donor and an acceptor is less than 10 nm. Consequently, it is reasonable to conclude that the complex is incapable of emitting chemiluminescence, while free N-CQDs remain after the binding reaction between Cu2+ and N-CQDs can emit vibrant chemiluminescence under the PO-CL reaction mechanism, as shown in Fig. 2(d).

We further investigated the dependence of the relative CL intensities on the concentration of N-CQDs in the absence and presence of 0.8 µM Cu2+, as depicted in Fig. 3(i). Notably, the relative CL intensity of 1600-fold diluted N-CQDs in the presence of 0.8 µM Cu2+ amounted to approximately 72% of the relative CL intensity observed with N-CQDs alone. With increasing concentration of N-CQDs, the ratio of relative CL intensities in the absence and presence of 0.8 µM Cu2+ proportionally decreases. Consequently, Fig. 3(i) signifies that a biosensor utilizing 1600-fold diluted N-CQDs demonstrates greater sensitivity compared to those employing higher concentrations of N-CQDs. However, as indicated in Fig. S2, the relative CL intensity (CL) measured in the presence of 1600-fold diluted N-CQDs was approximately 8.7-fold higher than the background (CLB) of the PO-CL reaction. These results suggest that a biosensor employing 1600-fold diluted N-CQDs may face limitations when quantifying relatively high concentrations of Cu2+ in human plasma or urine since the relative intensity calculated in the presence of high Cu2+ concentrations could fall as low as CLB. We opted for 800-fold diluted N-CQDs in biosensor development to establish a relatively broad linear calibration curve. In this configuration, CL/CLB reached as high as 14.5 (see Fig. S2), and the relative CL intensity of 800-fold diluted N-CQDs in the presence of 0.8 µM Cu2+ remained at 75% of the relative CL intensity of N-CQDs alone, as illustrated in Fig. 3(i).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of the binding reaction of N-CQDs and Cu2+ in human plasma
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. S3, the relative CL intensity of N-CQDs in human plasma remained unchanged compared to that in PBS (pH 7.4), contrary to our initial expectations, considering the presence of Cu2+ in human plasma. Furthermore, even when additional Cu2+ was introduced into human plasma, the relative CL intensity of N-CQDs did not exhibit any alteration. These observations suggest that N-CQDs are incapable of binding with Cu2+ in human plasma, especially within an environment rich in large-sized proteins and biomaterials.

To find out the concentration of Cu2+ in human plasma, we used the supernatant obtained after the centrifugation at 14,000 rpm for 5 min. Fig. 4 (a) displays the relative CL intensity in the supernatant was notably lower than that in PBS alone due to a specific concentration of Cu2+. To mitigate the interference from biomaterials in the supernatant, we employed a centrifugal filter (MWCO: 3K) to eliminate relatively small-sized biomaterials selectively. The relative CL intensity in the supernatant, filtered twice, matched that of the supernatant obtained following the centrifugation of human plasma. Consequently, we utilized supernatants derived from human plasma samples for the quantification of Cu2+ in human plasma. Fig. 4(b) demonstrates the direct quantification of Cu2+ in human urine, eliminating the need for time-consuming procedures such as centrifugation and filtration. Additionally, Fig. 4(c) confirms that N-CQDs can effectively bind with Cu2+ in supernatants within 5 min, verifying the feasibility of quantifying Cu2+ in human urine following a brief 5-minute incubation period [46,47].

Based on the preliminary data in Figs. 3 and 4, our biosensor effectively quantified trace amounts of Cu2+ in human plasma and urine samples. The relative CL intensity displayed an exponential decline with increasing Cu2+ concentration, as illustrated in Fig. 4(d). Furthermore, Fig. 4(e) demonstrates a linear calibration curve derived from the reciprocal of the relative CL intensity calculated in the presence of Cu2+. Remarkably, this linear calibration curve exhibited a broad dynamic range spanning from 0.05 to 3.8 µM, with meager low limits of detection (LOD=3 S/slope) as low as 19.5 nM, where 'S' signifies the standard deviation of the background (N=20).

Utilizing the linear calibration curve depicted in Fig. 4(e), our biosensor reliably quantified Cu2+ concentrations in human plasma and urine samples. Moreover, Table S1 provides compelling evidence that the sensitivity of our biosensor matches or even surpasses that of other established methodologies. Notably, our assay utilizing PO-CL detection proved comparably effective, even more, sensitive than biosensors employing CQDs and optical detection techniques such as chemiluminescence and fluorescence [48–50].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Accuracy, precision, recovery, and selectivity analysis of the biosensors
ce:section.ce:para: Table 1 encapsulates the remarkable performance of our biosensor in accurately, precisely, and reliably quantifying Cu2+ levels in human plasma samples. Across the plasma matrix, the biosensor exhibited accuracy ranging from 5.3% to 8.3%, alongside precision within the 3.6% to 10.5% range. Furthermore, its recovery rate in human plasma samples spanned from 90% to 107.1%. Notably, in human urine, the biosensor maintained commendable accuracy (4.4% to 7.5%), precision (8.2% to 12.5%), and recovery (94.4% to 107.5%), all well within statistically acceptable error limits. Fig. 4(f) and (g) provides visual evidence reinforcing the biosensor's capacity for precise quantitative measurement of Cu2+ with exceptional selectivity. This selectivity is attributed to the unique characteristic of N-CQDs, which do not engage in binding interactions with other heavy metals and primary constituents commonly found in human plasma and urine samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Correlation analysis of the novel biosensor and a conventional biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: The analytical findings derived from our innovative approach to quantifying Cu2+ in human plasma samples (n = 15) demonstrated a compelling correlation with results obtained via conventional means, as illustrated in Fig. 4(h). Likewise, the robust correlation observed between both methodologies in the quantification of Cu2+ in human urine samples (n = 15) is vividly depicted in Fig. 4(i). These results underscore the efficacy of our biosensor equipped with PO-CL detection as a cutting-edge analytical tool for accurate Cu2+ quantification in human samples, aligning seamlessly with the rigorous guidelines set forth by the EPA and NIH.

In this study, we synthesized N-CQDs using a novel microwave-assisted pyrolysis method involving ACD and EDA. These N-CQDs exhibited outstanding luminescent properties, acting as highly efficient emitters in the PO-CL reaction. The amine groups on their surface facilitated complexation with Cu2+, enabling energy transfer within the complex during the PO-CL reaction, following the intra-molecular chemiluminescence resonance energy transfer (intra-CRET) mechanism. Leveraging these properties, we developed a sensitive and selective biosensor for rapid Cu2+ detection in human plasma and urine, showing a strong correlation with standard methods and low error margins. This biosensor also promises Cu2+ monitoring in various water sources, including drinking water and natural bodies. We propose that the underlying principles of this biosensor could be applied to detecting various analytes with high accuracy, precision, and reproducibility. This biosensor offers an innovative approach to Cu2+ monitoring in human fluids and holds potential for broader analytical applications.
Additional data is available in the supporting information.

Seul-Yi Lee: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Nicole Sim: Validation, Software, Resources, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Jagadis Gautam: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Software, Resources. Young-Teck Kim: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Resources. Soo-Jin Park: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Ji Hoon Lee: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This project was performed as part of Luminescent MD, LLC's Research Program (LMD-201) and was supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Korea government (MSIT) (No. 2023R1A2C1004109 and 2022R1I1A1A01070007). Also, this was supported by Korea Energy (No. 2024-Research and Development in Field Technology, Yeongheung-01).
